# Billion Second Birthday Calculator

> Calculate exactly when you turn 1,000,000,000 seconds old. Enter your birth date, optional time, and timezone to discover your billionth second birthday — accurate to the second.

URL: https://billionbday.com/

## What This Tool Does

This is a free, client-side web calculator that computes the exact moment a person turns one billion (1,000,000,000) seconds old. One billion seconds is approximately 31 years, 251 days, 13 hours, 34 minutes, and 54 seconds.

Users enter their birth date (required), birth time (optional, defaults to midnight), and timezone (optional, defaults to the browser's timezone). The app then displays:

- A live counter showing the user's current age in seconds, updating in real time
- The exact date and time of their billionth second birthday
- A progress bar showing how far they are toward 1 billion seconds
- The number of UTC leap seconds that have occurred since their birth
- Whether the billionth second is in the past or future, and how far away it is

## Key Features

- **Leap second accuracy**: Accounts for all 27 UTC leap seconds inserted between 1972 and 2017.
- **Full timezone support**: Supports all IANA timezones via the browser's Intl API.
- **Calendar integration**: For future billionth second dates, users can add an event to Google Calendar or download an .ics file.
- **Sharing**: Users can share their billionth second date via the Web Share API or copy to clipboard.
- **Localized**: Available in 19 languages — English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Chinese, Arabic, Russian, Dutch, Hindi, Turkish, Swedish, Polish, Vietnamese, Thai, and Indonesian. Arabic uses RTL layout.
- **Accessible**: WCAG-compliant with ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support.
- **Privacy-first**: All computation happens in the browser. No data is sent to any server.

## How the Calculation Works

1. The user's birth date and time are converted to a UTC timestamp using the selected IANA timezone.
2. The app finds the instant T where elapsed Unix seconds from birth to T plus the count of UTC leap seconds in that interval equals 1,000,000,000 (same rule as the live age-in-seconds display).
3. That instant is the user's billionth second birthday.
4. The number of leap seconds since birth is also shown on its own as reference.

## Common Questions

- **How long is 1 billion seconds?** Approximately 31 years, 251 days, 13 hours, 34 minutes, and 54 seconds.
- **Who has already passed their billionth second?** Anyone born before roughly mid-1994 (as of 2026).
- **Does this account for leap years?** Yes — the calculation is timestamp-based, so leap years are inherently handled.
- **Does this account for leap seconds?** Yes — the billionth-second date matches the leap-adjusted age counter (Unix elapsed seconds plus inserted UTC leap seconds).
- **Is my data stored anywhere?** No. Everything runs client-side in your browser. No birth date or personal data is transmitted.

## Technical Details

- Built with Svelte 5, TypeScript, Vite, and Tailwind CSS v4.
- Single-page application, fully client-side rendered.
- No backend, no cookies, no analytics.
